evidence.page_title: Is This a Doctor Mourning a Woman Who Died During Childbirth?
evidence.title: Is This a Doctor Mourning a Woman Who Died During Childbirth?
evidence.description: A sketchy Facebook page claimed a photograph depicted "crying doctor" mourning for a patient who died in childbirth after struggling with fertility.
evidence.text:
On 5 September 2017, the Facebook page "Babies Are Beautiful" (@babofficial) posted a story accompanied by a photograph of a crying doctor. The narrative, purportedly penned by the unnamed doctor, told of a woman who gave birth under highly implausible medical circumstances, only to die after her child was delivered:
Today is the saddest day of my life.
As a Doctor, I have handled so many pregnant women in Labour and every time am in the delivery room I always pray to God to bless all mothers.
The pain women go through in the delivery room is undescribable and this does not include the 9 months they spent carrying the baby. They go through a lot just to bring forth new life.
Today I cried bitterly because I lost a woman, we don't pray for things like this to happen but sometimes God may have other plans. Why is this woman's case so painful? She has been barren for 14 years! We have tried IVF & so many method known to man, the woman went through a lot.
Finally God blessed her, it was way beyond science and human knowledge. She just got pregnant despite the fact she has ovarian cyst and huge load of fibroids, brethren she got pregnant. Her fibroid started melting and everything was OK, I know that's God, he will do things just to show off his glory and awesomeness.
After 9 months, it was time, her husband rushed her to the hospital and quickly I left everything that I was doing and attended to her. She laboured for hours, after 7 hours, it was so painful so we decided to open her up.
We lost her but the baby was alive,. Before her death, she held the baby in her arms and smiled "God is great" and then she gave up the ghost.
I was devastated and sad, I went to broke the news to her husband myself, upon hearing the news, her husband fainted, their happy day just turned sour. We lost a live just to deliver a new life today.
Please respect women because they pass through the valley of death to bring life. Respect your wife! Carrying your baby for 9 months is no jokes and labouring for hours to give birth to your children is a huge sacrifice.
I pray to God to please protect everyone reading this, especially pregnant women, please put them in your prayers. Dear husband, I repeat respect your wife because she is truly the giver of life. May God strengthen all pregnant women, you will all deliver your babies like the women of Hebrew.
Don't ignore this post, share to others it is very important because the women in our lives should be worshipped. Please if you are having problems with your mother and you refused to call her, I beg you to CALL HER NOW! she went through hell to give birth to you. Show some love to women, they are super. Please share.
GOD BLESS WOMEN ?
In just over 24 hours, the post was shared more than half a million times.
The Facebook page that posted the tale, "Babies Are Beautiful," appeared to be affiliated with an online baby supply shop (babbyy.com) which listed a California phone number and Delaware address as its contact information. However, the babbyy.com domain was registered by an owner in Nigeria.
The nature of the story suggested that the tale was fabricated to rack up Facebook likes and shares. (The majority of recent posts on the page were similar "likebait" photographs, sometimes directing users to "type amen.") No details were provided about the name of the doctor or location of the hospital, and commenters who identified themselves as medical professionals were quick to point out weak spots in the story:
Those commenters noted that, among other things, the purported patient would have been treated as high-risk and obtained a C-section, that uterine fibroids are not typically fatal, and that details of the account did not align with the actual origins of the photograph.
Özge Metin Photography was credited for the photograph of the crying doctor (which bears a watermark in Turkish) and posted it to Instagram on 6 September 2017. In the post's comment thread, photographer Özge Metin confirmed the "Babies Are Beautiful" post was a hoax, as the father captured in the image was crying tears of joy after the birth of his healthy baby:
kole.photography [link] Another stolen post. What a beautiful photo! You should get credit.
elia_79 Hi is this a sad story or it is just a hoax on internet
ozgemetinphotography @elia_79 This Crying Father is photo of mine, i took it. The Father was crying because his baby was born as a healthy baby. Not because his wife died, his wife did not die. This story is not true.
elia_79 @ozgemetinphotography ughh thank you so much. Yes I saw that it had your picture on it that's why I looked you up in Instagram thank you so much. Great picture
ozgemetinphotography@elia_79 ❤❤❤
We contacted babbyy.com and reached the owner, Alex Onyia, who told us that the photograph was not meant to depict the events in the story, which he said had been sent in by a fan of the Facebook page. He claimed that the company had reached out to photographer Özge Metin on Instagram and had secured permission to use her photographs, but given the Metin's comments on Instagram, we doubt that this is the case.
Onyia also maintained that the story was genuine, although he had not verified that the person who wrote it was an actual doctor: "We have featured so many similar stories over the years and all are genuine."
Although sharing the false story and misused photograph posed little danger to Facebook users, it did bolster the reach of a dubious Facebook page attached to a retail outlet of unknown reliability.

evidence.text:
Beloved Physician's Sudden Demise Leaves Medical Community in Mourning
Beloved Physician's Sudden Demise Leaves Medical Community in Mourning
The medical community is reeling in shock and sadness following the untimely death of Dr. Irene Say, a respected and beloved physician who dedicated her life to the care and well-being of her patients. Her sudden passing has sent ripples of grief throughout the medical community, with colleagues, patients, and friends paying tribute to her exceptional compassion, skill, and dedication to her craft. Dr. Say, who was just 45 years old, leaves behind a legacy of kindness, empathy, and unwavering commitment to her patients, and her loss will be deeply felt by all who knew her. As a family medicine physician, she was beloved by her patients, who praised her gentle touch, her patience, and her ability to listen and connect on a deep level.
Dr. Say's Medical Background and Career
Born and raised in the Philippines, Dr. Say moved to the United States to pursue her medical career, earning her medical degree from the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) David Geffen School of Medicine. She then completed her residency at the University of Washington Hospital and Clinics, where she honed her skills in family medicine and developed a special interest in chronic disease management. After years of working in a variety of clinical settings, Dr. Say settled into her role as a primary care physician at a community health clinic, where she spent the last decade of her life making a profound impact on the lives of her patients.
Colleagues and patients alike describe Dr. Say as an extraordinary person who radiated warmth, kindness, and compassion. "She was more than just a doctor; she was a guardian angel, a true friend, and a confidante," said Maria Rodriguez, a patient of Dr. Say's for over a decade. "She had a gift for putting people at ease, for listening without judgment, and for making them feel seen and heard." Dr. Say's colleagues praised her remarkable clinical skills, citing her ability to balance compassion with sound medical expertise. "Irene had a gift for connecting with her patients, for understanding their struggles, and for providing care that was tailored to their unique needs," said Dr. Thomas Kim, a fellow physician at the clinic where Dr. Say worked. "She was an outstanding clinician, but more importantly, she was an outstanding human being."
Dr. Say's Legacy and Impact
Dr. Say's influence extended far beyond the walls of the clinic, as she worked tirelessly to address systemic issues affecting her community. She was a vocal advocate for healthcare access and reform, and often spoke out on behalf of her patients to advocate for their needs. "Irene was a true champion of medical justice," said Emily Chen, a community organizer who worked with Dr. Say on various initiatives. "She understood the intersectionality of healthcare and social determinants of health, and she was committed to addressing the root causes of illness, not just treating the symptoms."
Dr. Say's Impact on Her Patients
For Dr. Say's patients, her passing is not just a loss of a medical provider, but a loss of a friend, a confidante, and a source of comfort. They remember her kindness, empathy, and genuine interest in their lives. "Dr. Say didn't just care about my blood pressure; she cared about me," said John Lee, a patient who credited Dr. Say with helping him manage his hypertension. "She would take the time to listen to my concerns, to ask questions, and to offer words of encouragement when I needed it most." Maria Rodriguez reflected on the countless late nights and weekends when Dr. Say stayed late to work, ensuring that her patients had access to the medical care they needed. "She was a guardian angel, a true hero, and a shining example of compassion and empathy in action."
Community Tributes and Condolences
The medical community is coming together to pay tribute to Dr. Say's remarkable life and legacy. The clinic where she worked has set up a memorial fund in her name, dedicated to supporting patient scholarships and healthcare initiatives. In addition, a number of community leaders and organizations have issued statements expressing their shock and sadness at her passing. "Irene was a true leader, a good soul, and a selfless servant who put the needs of others before her own," said John Tai, Executive Director of the Asian American Citizens of America (AACA). "Her loss will be deeply felt, but her legacy will live on through the countless lives she touched and the impact she made on our community."
Dr. Say's Death Raises Awareness about Burnout and Mental Health
Dr. Say's untimely death has also highlighted the importance of addressing burnout and mental health in the medical community. As a high-stress occupation, medicine can take a toll on even the most dedicated and compassionate providers. "We must acknowledge the risks and realities of burnout, and do more to support our healthcare professionals," said Dr. Alexis Karamanides, President of the Massachusetts Medical Society. "We must prioritize self-care, mental health, and colleague support systems to prevent the kind of tragic loss we are witnessing today."
Family and Friends Mourn the Loss of a Beloved Daughter, Friend, and Colleague
Dr. Say is survived by her loving family, including her husband and two young children. Friends and colleagues recall her infectious laughter, her warm smile, and her unwavering optimism. "Irene was the kind of person who made you feel seen, heard, and valued," said friend and colleague, Dr. Maria Vasquez. "Her kindness, empathy, and compassion inspired us all to be better versions of ourselves, and her loss leaves a huge void in our lives." Dr. Rafael Castellanos added, "Her passing has shocked us to our core, and we will miss her dearly – her family, her friends, and her patients – the fabric of our community will never be the same without her presence."
Conclusion
Dr. Irene Say will be deeply missed, but her legacy will continue to inspire and motivate the medical community to provide care with compassion, understanding, and empathy. She was a shining example of the highest standards of medical practice, and her dedication to her patients, her colleagues, and her community will not be forgotten.

evidence.text:
Beloved Physician's Sudden Demise Leaves Medical Community in Mourning
Beloved Physician's Sudden Demise Leaves Medical Community in Mourning
The medical community is reeling in shock and sadness following the untimely death of Dr. Irene Say, a respected and beloved physician who dedicated her life to the care and well-being of her patients. Her sudden passing has sent ripples of grief throughout the medical community, with colleagues, patients, and friends paying tribute to her exceptional compassion, skill, and dedication to her craft. Dr. Say, who was just 45 years old, leaves behind a legacy of kindness, empathy, and unwavering commitment to her patients, and her loss will be deeply felt by all who knew her. As a family medicine physician, she was beloved by her patients, who praised her gentle touch, her patience, and her ability to listen and connect on a deep level.
Dr. Say's Medical Background and Career
Born and raised in the Philippines, Dr. Say moved to the United States to pursue her medical career, earning her medical degree from the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) David Geffen School of Medicine. She then completed her residency at the University of Washington Hospital and Clinics, where she honed her skills in family medicine and developed a special interest in chronic disease management. After years of working in a variety of clinical settings, Dr. Say settled into her role as a primary care physician at a community health clinic, where she spent the last decade of her life making a profound impact on the lives of her patients.
Colleagues and patients alike describe Dr. Say as an extraordinary person who radiated warmth, kindness, and compassion. "She was more than just a doctor; she was a guardian angel, a true friend, and a confidante," said Maria Rodriguez, a patient of Dr. Say's for over a decade. "She had a gift for putting people at ease, for listening without judgment, and for making them feel seen and heard." Dr. Say's colleagues praised her remarkable clinical skills, citing her ability to balance compassion with sound medical expertise. "Irene had a gift for connecting with her patients, for understanding their struggles, and for providing care that was tailored to their unique needs," said Dr. Thomas Kim, a fellow physician at the clinic where Dr. Say worked. "She was an outstanding clinician, but more importantly, she was an outstanding human being."
Dr. Say's Legacy and Impact
Dr. Say's influence extended far beyond the walls of the clinic, as she worked tirelessly to address systemic issues affecting her community. She was a vocal advocate for healthcare access and reform, and often spoke out on behalf of her patients to advocate for their needs. "Irene was a true champion of medical justice," said Emily Chen, a community organizer who worked with Dr. Say on various initiatives. "She understood the intersectionality of healthcare and social determinants of health, and she was committed to addressing the root causes of illness, not just treating the symptoms."
Dr. Say's Impact on Her Patients
For Dr. Say's patients, her passing is not just a loss of a medical provider, but a loss of a friend, a confidante, and a source of comfort. They remember her kindness, empathy, and genuine interest in their lives. "Dr. Say didn't just care about my blood pressure; she cared about me," said John Lee, a patient who credited Dr. Say with helping him manage his hypertension. "She would take the time to listen to my concerns, to ask questions, and to offer words of encouragement when I needed it most." Maria Rodriguez reflected on the countless late nights and weekends when Dr. Say stayed late to work, ensuring that her patients had access to the medical care they needed. "She was a guardian angel, a true hero, and a shining example of compassion and empathy in action."
Community Tributes and Condolences
The medical community is coming together to pay tribute to Dr. Say's remarkable life and legacy. The clinic where she worked has set up a memorial fund in her name, dedicated to supporting patient scholarships and healthcare initiatives. In addition, a number of community leaders and organizations have issued statements expressing their shock and sadness at her passing. "Irene was a true leader, a good soul, and a selfless servant who put the needs of others before her own," said John Tai, Executive Director of the Asian American Citizens of America (AACA). "Her loss will be deeply felt, but her legacy will live on through the countless lives she touched and the impact she made on our community."
Dr. Say's Death Raises Awareness about Burnout and Mental Health
Dr. Say's untimely death has also highlighted the importance of addressing burnout and mental health in the medical community. As a high-stress occupation, medicine can take a toll on even the most dedicated and compassionate providers. "We must acknowledge the risks and realities of burnout, and do more to support our healthcare professionals," said Dr. Alexis Karamanides, President of the Massachusetts Medical Society. "We must prioritize self-care, mental health, and colleague support systems to prevent the kind of tragic loss we are witnessing today."
Family and Friends Mourn the Loss of a Beloved Daughter, Friend, and Colleague
Dr. Say is survived by her loving family, including her husband and two young children. Friends and colleagues recall her infectious laughter, her warm smile, and her unwavering optimism. "Irene was the kind of person who made you feel seen, heard, and valued," said friend and colleague, Dr. Maria Vasquez. "Her kindness, empathy, and compassion inspired us all to be better versions of ourselves, and her loss leaves a huge void in our lives." Dr. Rafael Castellanos added, "Her passing has shocked us to our core, and we will miss her dearly – her family, her friends, and her patients – the fabric of our community will never be the same without her presence."
Conclusion
Dr. Irene Say will be deeply missed, but her legacy will continue to inspire and motivate the medical community to provide care with compassion, understanding, and empathy. She was a shining example of the highest standards of medical practice, and her dedication to her patients, her colleagues, and her community will not be forgotten.
